one_line_takeaway: A client-side mask-then-unmask pipeline that strips PII from legal prompts before they reach an external LLM, reaching 93% (GLiNER) to 97% (Qwen2.5-14B) F1 on PII detection over 50 synthetic immigration prompts while preserving ~0.97-0.98 cosine output fidelity, but validated only on small synthetic single-domain data with no adversarial leakage test.
key_finding: Both backends detect PII with high accuracy, with a precision/recall trade-off. Overall (Figure 3): GLiNER reached 100% precision, 88% recall, 93% F1; Qwen2.5-14B reached 99% precision, 94% recall, 97% F1. GLiNER over-masks less (perfect precision) while Qwen catches more entities (higher recall), especially context-dependent ones. Masking/unmasking barely degraded output meaning: cosine similarity to unmasked-prompt outputs was 0.9808 (GLiNER) and 0.9731 (Qwen). The authors conclude a hybrid (GLiNER primary for precision, Qwen supplementary for recall) best balances the privacy-utility trade-off.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LegalGuardian, a lightweight client-side privacy-preserving framework that lets lawyers use external LLM chatbots without exposing client PII. Its own contributions are: (1) a four-stage pipeline (synthetic data generation, PII masking layer, secure prompting layer, evaluation layer) that detects PII in a prompt, replaces each entity with a consistent numbered placeholder such as [PERSON 1]/[ADDRESS 1], sends only the masked prompt to the external LLM, then unmasks placeholders in the returned output using a maintained entity dictionary; (2) a released synthetic dataset of 50 immigration-law prompts (containing 460 entities across 10 PII categories); and (3) a comparative evaluation of two interchangeable masking backends: the GLiNER Multi PII-v1 NER model versus a locally hosted Qwen2.5-14B LLM prompted one-shot for NER. The authors position it as the first privacy framework explicitly designed for attorney-client confidentiality in the generative-AI era, and recommend a hybrid GLiNER-primary + Qwen-supplementary configuration. Cryptographic (MPC/HE), differential-privacy, federated-learning, EmojiCrypt, PP-TS (Kan et al. 2023) and Hide-and-Seek (Chen et al. 2023) approaches are discussed in Related Work as prior art, NOT as this paper's contribution or as empirical baselines.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious / untrusted third-party (cloud) LLM provider. A lawyer must paste sensitive client PII (names, addresses, nationality, case numbers, tax IDs, dates of birth) into prompts for tasks like summarization, translation, legal analysis, and drafting. Sending that PII to an external LLM provider risks unauthorized exposure, breaching attorney-client privilege, ABA Model Rule 1.6, and data-protection law; the paper cites the March 2023 OpenAI Redis bug that leaked users' chat histories as a concrete precedent. The defense is purely client-side: all PII is masked before any external interaction, so the external LLM only ever sees placeholders. The framework does not defend against a compromised local machine, malicious local model, or an adversary attempting active re-identification of masked entities; residual leakage from imperfect recall is not adversarially tested.
limitations: Evaluation is small and narrow: 50 synthetic prompts, 460 entities, single domain (US immigration law), predominantly English, no real case data and no user study with practicing lawyers. The 'external' LLM is simulated by a second local Qwen2.5-14B instance, so no real third-party provider is exercised. The semantic-similarity baseline is itself Qwen-generated, making the utility metric somewhat self-referential. Critically for a privacy claim, recall is below 100% (GLiNER misses ~12% of entities, Qwen ~6%), meaning some real PII passes to the external model, yet the paper does not adversarially test residual leakage or re-identification risk from unmasked entities or from placeholder structure. No empirical comparison is made against the prior sanitization frameworks it cites (PP-TS, Hide-and-Seek, EmojiCrypt); the only comparison is internal (GLiNER vs Qwen). Latency/cost of running a local 14B model on typical solo-practitioner hardware is asserted as 'lightweight' but not measured.

datasets_used: Self-generated synthetic dataset of 50 immigration-law prompts (Faker + Qwen2.5-14B), containing 460 entities across 10 PII categories (person, case number, date of birth, address, company, tax ID, location, date, law office, nationality); authors state intent to release it
quantitative_results: Entity-level person: GLiNER P1.00/R0.72/F1 0.84; Qwen P1.00/R0.77/F1 0.87
Entity-level location: GLiNER P1.00/R0.65/F1 0.79; Qwen P1.00/R1.00/F1 1.00
Entity-level address: GLiNER P1.00/R0.98/F1 0.99; Qwen P0.99/R1.00/F1 0.99
Perfect (1.00/1.00/1.00) F1 for both models on tax id, case number, and law office
Semantic cosine similarity to unmasked-prompt outputs: GLiNER 0.9808, Qwen2.5-14B 0.9731
Levenshtein distance: GLiNER 0.4358, Qwen2.5-14B 0.4017 (Qwen lower = fewer char-level changes)
baselines_compared: Internal comparison of the two masking backends: GLiNER Multi PII-v1 vs one-shot-prompted Qwen2.5-14B
Semantic-similarity baseline: outputs from original unmasked prompts (generated by Qwen2.5-14B) vs masked-then-unmasked pipeline outputs
No empirical comparison against prior frameworks (PP-TS, Hide-and-Seek, EmojiCrypt, MPC/HE, DP, federated learning) — these appear only in Related Work
